title: How the 2016 Nice terrorist attack unfolded, minute-by-minute
subhed: On July 14, 2016, a terrorist drove a truck into crowds gathered on the famed Promenade des Anglais in the southern French city, killing 86 people. Ten years later, Le Monde looks back at how the tragedy unfolded.

Ten years after the July 14 terrorist attack, Nice struggles with a painful process of remembrance
subhed: The city has found it difficult to convey the horror that left 86 people dead and more than 400 injured in 2016. Many victims feel that commemorations are 'second rate.'

title: US immigration agents involved in another fatal shooting
subhed: An ICE agent fatally shot a 26-year-old Colombian man in the town of Biddeford, Maine, on Monday – only one week after a different ICE agent shot and killed a Mexican man in Texas.

title: 'For France, everything is so easy at this World Cup that it's almost unsettling'
subhed: The French team have been so dominant at the World Cup that their supporters need to beware of excessive optimism, writes columnist Jérôme Latta.

title: 'And I thought coke was for stressed-out executives!': Cocaine's ravages among manual workers
subhed: 'Cocaine at work' (3/3). Long associated with elite, urban circles, cocaine has now made its way deep into rural France and its mid-sized towns. On construction sites, farms and in warehouses, workers, some barely out of their teens, are turning to the drug to get through the physical toll of their jobs.

subhed: The deals that US President Donald Trump so readily boasts about seemed destined to get bogged down in the Middle East right from the start.

title: 'If they catch me, it's over for me': Senegal's LGBTQ+ community lives in daily fear of repression
subhed: In March, Senegal passed a law increasing penalties for same-sex relations, leaving LGBTQ+ people living in fear. Several described their deeply uncertain daily lives, gripped by the constant anxiety of being arrested.

title: Silicon Valley's next generation feels the AI blues
subhed: In California, the rise of artificial intelligence no longer generates the unanimous enthusiasm it once did. Anxiety is mounting, especially among students and engineers, as jobs have been cut and democratic risks have grown more threatening.
